title: I Cry Out with My Heart
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: I Cry Out with my Heart - Psalm 119_145-152 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Repetition in Psalm 119 Is Not Accidental
subpoints: After today, only three stanzas remain in Psalm 119 — this is near the end of the series
A fellow pastor asked how one preaches through Psalm 119 when it says the same thing every eight verses — the repetition is because we need to hear it
We are slow to learn, slow to believe, and quick to despair — the Lord is patient and keeps telling us what we need
Four observations in this stanza: the cries of the Psalmist, the problem he faces, the help he finds, and the purpose he declares

outline.title: 1. The Cries of the Psalmist — Urgent, Wholehearted Prayer
subpoints: Three times the Psalmist cries: 'I cry with my whole heart' (v.145); 'I cry out to you' (v.146); 'I cry for help' (v.147) — these are not casual prayers but screams of desperation
Even our Lord Jesus was urgent in prayer — Hebrews 5:7: 'In the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with vehement cries and tears'

William Plummer: 'We must not only pray but pray without ceasing; not only cry with the voice but with the holy vehemence of the soul; from the whole heart; with the attitude of Jacob: I will not let you go until you bless me'
Our lack of comfort in trouble is often due to our lack of urgency in prayer — 'Your soul would not be so empty of comfort if your mouth were not so empty of prayer'

outline.title: 2. The Problem — The Enemy Is Drawing Near
subpoints: Verse 150: 'They draw near who follow after wickedness; they are far from your law' — persecutors with evil purposes are pressing in from every direction
The very reason they draw near with evil purposes is because they are far from the law of God — the enemy is near because the enemy is far from the Word
As society moves further from the Word of God, believers can expect those opposed to God to draw nearer with evil purposes
We should not be surprised — if the Psalmist had to undergo these troubles, so should we in our times

outline.title: 3. The Help — God Is Nearer Than the Enemy
subpoints: Verse 151: 'You are near, O Lord, and all your commandments are truth' — when trouble draws near, God is even closer
Psalm 23: 'Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil — for you are with me' — when trouble is near, God is nearer

Verse 151-152: the Psalmist gives three reasons to hope in God: God is near; God's word is truthful ('all your commandments are truth'); God does not change ('I have known of old that you have founded them forever')
Those who persecute believers are the ones truly in danger — whether they know it or not, the Lord is near his people and the angels of the Lord encamp around them

outline.title: 4. The Purpose — Devotion to the Word in Response to Deliverance
subpoints: Verses 145-147: the Psalmist declares his purpose — 'I will keep your statutes' (v.145); 'I will keep your testimonies' (v.146); 'I hope in your word' (v.147)
God's deliverance fuels his desire to live as a sacrifice of praise — the response to the Lord's mercy is a life devoted to his Word
He will keep the statutes, observe the testimonies, hope in the Word, and meditate on the promises
Our response to the Lord's mercy and deliverance in times of trouble must be that we purpose to live as a sacrifice of praise

summary:
Preaching from the Qoph stanza of Psalm 119 — verses 145-152 — near the end of the series, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses the pattern of urgency and comfort that runs through the Psalmist's prayers. The apparent repetition of Psalm 119 is not a literary failure but a theological necessity: we are slow to learn, slow to believe, and quick to despair, and the Lord patiently keeps pressing the same truths home.

Four elements structure the passage: the cries of the Psalmist, the problem he faces, the help he finds, and the purpose he declares. His cries are not polite petitions but screams of whole-hearted desperation — echoing even the prayer life of Jesus himself, who in the days of his flesh offered prayers with vehement cries and tears (Hebrews 5:7). William Plummer's diagnosis applies as sharply today: our souls are empty of comfort because our mouths are empty of prayer.

The problem is enemies pressing near with evil purposes — persecutors who are far from the Word of God. But the great reversal is verse 151: 'You are near, O Lord.' When danger draws near, God is nearer still. As in Psalm 23 and the story of Elisha's servant seeing the armies of heaven, God's people are never outnumbered. Three reasons anchor the Psalmist's hope: God is near, his Word is true, and he does not change. The stanza ends with a declaration of purpose — the Psalmist commits to keeping God's statutes, meditating on his Word, and hoping in his promises — the natural response of a heart that has been delivered.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Repetition in Psalm 119 Is Not Accidental

After today there will be three stanzas left and we will be done with Psalm 119. We are on the Qoph section — verse 145.

Psalm 119, verse 145, reading through 152:

*I cry out with my whole heart; hear me, O Lord. I will keep your statutes. I cry out to you; save me, and I will keep your testimonies. I rise before the dawning of the morning and cry for help; I hope in your word. My eyes are awake through the night watches, that I may meditate on your word. Hear my voice according to your lovingkindness; O Lord, revive me according to your justice. They draw near who follow after wickedness; they are far from your law. You are near, O Lord, and all your commandments are truth. Concerning your testimonies, I have known of old that you have founded them forever.*

As we look at this passage, you might say: this sounds familiar. Is the Psalmist again in trouble crying out for help? This is getting a little repetitious. And that can be the impression. Yet, as we go through Psalm 119 week after week, one of the pastors who was preaching nearby asked me, 'How do you do that? Because it seems like you have been talking about the same thing every eight verses.'

And there is a lot of the Word of God, there is a central theme of the Word of God, there is repetition there. But if you think the repetition is unnecessary, just remember that the Lord is very patient with us. He says things over and over again and we tend not to listen.

There is a story of a pastor who came as the first pastor of a church and preached a great sermon — everybody was excited. The second Sunday he preached the same sermon. People were a little shocked but it was still a great sermon. Third Sunday, same sermon. Fourth Sunday, same sermon again. Finally people went to him: 'You have been preaching the same sermon every Sunday.' And he said, 'Well, you haven't changed yet, so I will keep repeating this one.'

The Lord does that to us. He keeps on telling us things again and again even though we refuse to hear. So if there is repetition in Psalm 119, it is because we need to hear what the Lord is telling us so that we can believe. We are slow to learn. We are slow to believe. We are quick to despair. So the Lord has these cries for us to see here in his Word.

I want us to see four things in this stanza: the cries of the Psalmist; the problem the Psalmist is dealing with; the help the Psalmist finds; and the purpose the Psalmist declares.

## 1. The Cries of the Psalmist

Once again the Psalmist is teaching us how to find comfort in trouble — and he points especially to God's precious Word. Psalm 119 is about suffering and the Word of God. As we consider the comfort we get in trouble from God's Word, I want us to see the cries the Psalmist lifts up to the Lord.

In verse 145: 'I cry with my whole heart.' In verse 146: 'I cry out to you.' In verse 147: 'I cry for help.' These cries are not casual. The word 'cry' here is actually screaming — bringing your words before God with great desperation and urgency. We see in the Psalmist's prayer: urgency. Intensity. This is not a polished, composed prayer. He is urgently and desperately calling out for the Lord.

Verse 146: 'Save me.' Verse 147: 'Help me.' He is praying like this because he is in a desperate situation. In verse 150 he says, 'They draw near who follow after wickedness.' The enemy is coming close — and so he cries to the Lord.

So let me ask you: are you urgent in times of trouble in your prayers? Are you coming before the Lord crying like this Psalmist in your times of trouble? Do you know that even our Lord Jesus himself was urgent in times of trouble in prayer? Hebrews 5:7, speaking about Christ: 'In the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with vehement cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death — and was heard because of his godly fear.' Even our Savior, in the time of trouble and agony, lifted up his voice not calmly but with loud cries and tears. And he was heard. How much more do we need to pray in that way? Brothers and sisters, if our Savior did that, how much more should we?

William Plummer, who wrote a commentary on the Psalms, says this about this passage: 'In supplication, we must not only call upon God but continue to call upon God. We must not only pray, but pray without ceasing. We must not only cry with the voice, but with the holy vehemence of the soul. We must not only pray with the heart, but from the whole heart. Our attitude must be the attitude of Jacob: I will not let you go until you bless me.'

Remember the story of Jacob in Genesis — he wrestled with God all night and when God said, 'Let me go,' Jacob said, 'I am not going to let you go until you bless me.' That is the kind of urgency, the vehemence, with which this Psalmist comes. These are good words to remind us that so often our lack of comfort in trouble is because of our lack of urgency in prayer. Plummer also says: 'Your soul would not be so empty of comfort if your mouth were not so empty of prayer.'

May these words be an encouragement to us to be urgent, to be persistent, to be desperate, to be vehement in prayer.

## 2. The Problem — The Enemy Is Drawing Near

In verse 150 we see the problem the Psalmist faces: 'They draw near who follow after wickedness; they are far from your law.' What is the problem? They draw near. Who are they? The persecutors who have evil plans and evil purposes. This is what is driving the urgency of the Psalmist's prayer.

As believers, especially in times when God's Word is not honored — and our times are those times — we should not be surprised to find ourselves in these kinds of circumstances. If the Psalmist had to undergo these troubles in Bible times, it should not surprise us to have to undergo them in ours. They are pressing from every direction.

Notice the interesting connection of words in verse 150. They draw near — and they are far. 'They draw near who follow after wickedness; they are far from your law.' The very reason they are drawing near to persecute him with evil purposes is because they are far from the law of God. Because they are far from the Word of God, they do not honor the Word of God, and they engage in evil purposes against God's people.

So the more our society moves away from the Word of God, the more those opposed to God will draw near to us with evil purposes. There is a correlation between those two things.

## 3. The Help — God Is Even Nearer

But there is hope. Look at verse 151: 'You are near, O Lord, and all your commandments are truth.' When trouble draws near, God is even closer than trouble to his people.

Remember how Psalm 23 goes: 'Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil — why? For you are with me.' When trouble is near, God is nearer.

And so let me ask you: do you believe that when those who do not love the Lord draw near to do harm, they are the ones who are really in trouble? They are the ones truly in danger — because, whether they realize it or not, the Lord is near you. You believe that? If people are persecuting you, they are the ones in danger, not you — because the Lord is near to protect you. Remember the story of Elisha: armies came and surrounded him. And though that army could not see the angels of the Lord surrounding them, the Lord opened the eyes of Elisha's servant to see it. That is the same with us. The angels of the Lord encamp around those that belong to the Lord.

In verses 151-152, the Psalmist gives three reasons why he hopes in God as his help. He has already lifted up his prayer in verse 149 on the basis of two things: God's covenant faithfulness, and God's justice — these wicked people deny your Word; they need to be judged.

Here in 151 and 152, three more reasons:

**First:** God is near. 'You are near, O Lord' — verse 151. So he prays confidently to the God who is near. Our God is near.

**Second:** God's Word is truthful — verse 151: 'All your commandments are truth.' The Word of God is true because God himself is true. He is not a man that he should lie. His words may be trusted because he can be trusted.

**Third:** God does not change — verse 152: 'Concerning your testimonies, I have known of old that you have founded them forever.' God's Word is unchanging because God is unchanging. Because of that, the Psalmist has hope that God is going to help him.

## 4. The Purpose — Devotion to the Word in Response to Deliverance

In verses 145, 146, and 147, the Psalmist — in gratitude for God's deliverance — declares that he wants to keep God's Word and meditate on his promise.

Verse 145: 'I will keep your statutes.' Verse 146: 'I will keep your testimonies.' Verse 147: 'I hope in your word.' The Psalmist is going to devote himself to the study of God's Word. And he is going to devote himself to the practice of God's Word — he is going to worship God in Word, keep the statutes, observe the testimonies, hope in the Word, meditate on his promises.

God's deliverance of the Psalmist fuels his desire to live as a sacrifice of praise. So his purpose is: I am going to obey your Word because you are my help and my hope in times of trouble. As you answer my cry, I am going to purpose to live according to your Word.

The Psalmist is saying: our response to the Lord's mercy and deliverance in a time of trouble must be that we want to live a life as a sacrifice of praise because he has delivered us. This whole section of the Psalm teaches us once again how to find comfort in the midst of danger, and how to find hope when we need help. And every believer needs that just about all the time. We all need help. We all need hope.

So we cry out to the Lord who is near. This is the section, the words of the Psalmist — but they are also our words. We can cry out to the Lord knowing he is going to be our help and our hope, purposing to obey him.

Father, thank you for your Word. We pray that it would penetrate deeply into our hearts that we might live in it. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
